theme: Diagnosing metric drops and root cause analysis
objective: Build a full RCA tree for a specific dashboard anomaly, identify the most likely root cause from a set of data signals, and list 3 prioritised next data checks with the reasoning behind each.

realWorldAnchor.example: A Monday morning order volume drop at Swiggy is one of the most commonly cited PM interview scenarios in India — it became a reference case because Swiggy PMs reportedly use this exact type of dashboard anomaly during on-call rotations, and the structured diagnosis approach (funnel stage isolation, platform split, geography split, time-of-day split) is a repeatable playbook across Indian consumer internet companies.

teamContext: Metric drop diagnosis is the PM's primary interface with the Data team during an incident — the PM's job is to generate and prioritise hypotheses, while the analyst runs the queries. A PM who can specify exactly what data check to run next (rather than asking 'can you check the data?') compresses the diagnosis loop from days to hours.

tasks.title: A Framework for Diagnosing Metric Drops — Lenny's Newsletter
tasks.summary: This article lays out a structured step-by-step playbook for diagnosing a product metric drop — covering how to isolate the affected funnel stage, how to split by platform and geography, and how to differentiate between internal (product/engineering) and external (market/competitive) causes. For Pranav, this is the operational version of Week 1's MECE tree: a specific diagnosis playbook rather than a general thinking method.
keyTakeaways: The first question in any metric drop is: which part of the funnel is affected? A drop in orders with flat sessions means the problem is in the conversion funnel (activation or checkout); a drop in both orders and sessions means the problem is upstream (acquisition or a major bug causing users to not even open the app)

The second split is always platform: iOS vs Android vs web. A drop isolated to one platform almost always points to a recent code release, an OS-level change, or a platform-specific infrastructure issue — if both platforms drop together, the cause is product-level or external

Time-of-day and geography splits are the two most underused diagnostic moves in India specifically: Indian metro vs Tier 2 data often moves differently during regional holidays, cricket match evenings, and salary-day cycles — a PM who only looks at national numbers will miss a city-level or time-specific anomaly

tasks.title: Root Cause Analysis for Product Managers — Product School
tasks.summary: This article explains how to apply the root cause analysis (RCA) method specifically in a product context — using the 5 Whys technique and fishbone diagrams alongside funnel data to move from a symptom ('orders dropped 18%') to a root cause ('Android app version 3.2.1 introduced a payment step regression that broke checkout for 31% of Android users'). The article bridges Day 2's MECE tree thinking with Day 8's metric framework.
keyTakeaways: RCA in product management has a specific structure: start with the symptom (the metric that moved), isolate the affected segment (funnel stage, platform, geography, cohort), form a specific hypothesis, verify it with one targeted data check, then iterate — this is faster than building an exhaustive tree before running any data

The 5 Whys technique is most useful once you have identified the affected segment: 'Orders dropped' → 'Android orders dropped' → 'Android checkout completion dropped' → 'Android payment step has a new error state' → 'The v3.2.1 release changed the payment SDK timeout parameter' — each 'why' narrows the scope and points to a specific fix

The most common RCA mistake in PM interviews is confusing correlation with causation: a Swiggy PM who sees that order drop correlates with rain might conclude rain caused the drop, when actually a delivery partner availability drop (caused by rain) caused the drop — the mechanism matters for the fix

tasks.keyword: Swiggy order drop root cause analysis Monday product analytics diagnosis India PM interview case
tasks.whyItMatters: The Swiggy Monday order drop is the most referenced PM interview case in the Indian tech hiring circuit — searching for written walkthroughs of this specific case gives Pranav exposure to how experienced PMs structure the same problem he will solve in today's assessment. Look for PM prep blogs, YouTube walkthroughs, or LinkedIn posts that walk through this exact scenario step by step.
whatToLearn: What the standard diagnosis sequence looks like for this case: funnel isolation first (sessions vs conversion vs checkout), then platform split, then geography split, then time-of-day, then external factors — and why this sequence is not arbitrary but follows the logic of what each split can rule out
What the top hypothesis typically turns out to be in worked examples of this case, and what data check would confirm or reject it within 30 minutes of starting the investigation

tasks.promptTitle: SQL parallel — read query output table, identify the anomaly
tasks.claudePrompt:
You are a senior PM helping me practice reading SQL query output to identify data anomalies — a core PM skill when working with analysts during a metric drop investigation.

Today I studied:
- How to diagnose a metric drop: funnel stage isolation, platform split, geography split, time-of-day split
- Root cause analysis: 5 Whys technique, moving from symptom to root cause through targeted data checks
- Why India-specific patterns (city-level drops, day-of-week cycles, platform splits) require specific diagnostic moves

Real world context: I am an APM at Swiggy. My analyst ran the following query and shared the output with me. I need to find the anomaly before the Monday morning leadership standup.

The query:
```sql
SELECT
  DATE(created_at)    AS order_date,
  delivery_city       AS city,
  COUNT(order_id)     AS total_orders
FROM orders
WHERE order_status IN ('completed', 'cancelled')
  AND created_at BETWEEN '2024-01-01' AND '2024-01-14'
GROUP BY DATE(created_at), delivery_city
ORDER BY order_date, city;
```

The output table:

| order_date | city | total_orders |
|---|---|---|
| 2024-01-01 (Mon) | Bangalore | 9,210 |
| 2024-01-01 (Mon) | Delhi | 7,840 |
| 2024-01-01 (Mon) | Mumbai | 14,320 |
| 2024-01-02 (Tue) | Bangalore | 9,450 |
| 2024-01-02 (Tue) | Delhi | 7,910 |
| 2024-01-02 (Tue) | Mumbai | 14,580 |
| 2024-01-03 (Wed) | Bangalore | 9,380 |
| 2024-01-03 (Wed) | Delhi | 7,870 |
| 2024-01-03 (Wed) | Mumbai | 14,430 |
| 2024-01-07 (Sun) | Bangalore | 11,020 |
| 2024-01-07 (Sun) | Delhi | 9,330 |
| 2024-01-07 (Sun) | Mumbai | 17,210 |
| 2024-01-08 (Mon) | Bangalore | 9,190 |
| 2024-01-08 (Mon) | Delhi | 2,140 |
| 2024-01-08 (Mon) | Mumbai | 14,350 |
| 2024-01-09 (Tue) | Bangalore | 9,430 |
| 2024-01-09 (Tue) | Delhi | 7,890 |
| 2024-01-09 (Tue) | Mumbai | 14,490 |
| 2024-01-14 (Sun) | Bangalore | 10,980 |
| 2024-01-14 (Sun) | Delhi | 9,290 |
| 2024-01-14 (Sun) | Mumbai | 17,180 |

Your role today: Act as my analyst. Answer my questions directly. If I identify the wrong anomaly, correct me. If I jump to a hypothesis before isolating the anomaly precisely, stop me and ask me to describe the data pattern first.

Activity — 3 steps:
Step 1: I identify the anomaly in the data — which row(s), what the number is, what it should be, and how I calculated the expected value.
Step 2: I state my top hypothesis for what caused it — with one sentence of reasoning.
Step 3: I name the single most important data check I would run next to confirm or reject the hypothesis, and explain why this check (not another) comes first.

After all 3 steps, give feedback in EXACTLY this format:
WHAT WORKED: [bullets]
WHAT MISSED: [bullets]
INDIA-SPECIFIC NOTE: [one insight — January 8, 2024 was a Monday in the week after Makar Sankranti / Lohri, which is a regional holiday celebrated heavily in Delhi and North India — this is a real India-specific diagnostic clue that a non-India PM would miss entirely]
COACHING NOTE: [one specific improvement tip for Pranav on reading data tables quickly]

Begin with: 'Good. Look at the output table carefully. Step 1: identify the anomaly. Tell me which specific city, which specific date, what the actual number is, what you would expect it to be based on the surrounding data, and how large the deviation is in percentage terms.'

bonusTasks.title: PM incident diagnosis playbook — Indian consumer internet
bonusTasks.keyword: product manager metric drop investigation playbook on-call incident India analytics Swiggy Zepto Razorpay 2023
bonusTasks.summary: Searching for how Indian PMs describe their incident diagnosis process — the actual steps they follow when a metric drops on a Monday morning — gives Pranav a practitioner-level view of what 'good' looks like in the role he is preparing for. Look for LinkedIn posts, PM blogs, or podcast clips where Indian PMs describe their weekly data review ritual and what happens when numbers go wrong.

bonusTasks.promptTitle: Extend the RCA — what if the hypothesis is wrong?
bonusTasks.claudePrompt:
You are a senior PM running a post-incident debrief with me after I completed a metric drop RCA.

Context: I identified that Delhi orders dropped 73% on January 8, 2024 (Monday after Makar Sankranti / Lohri) and hypothesised that this was caused by a regional holiday reducing demand.

Your role today: Play devil's advocate. My hypothesis might be right — but it might be wrong or incomplete. Push me to consider alternative explanations and ask me: if the 'regional holiday' hypothesis turns out to be false (for example, if the other Delhi-heavy holidays in our data do NOT show similar drops), what would my backup hypothesis be? What would that tell me about the root cause?

Activity:
Step 1: I state my backup hypothesis (if holiday demand reduction is ruled out).
Step 2: I describe the data check that would distinguish between the holiday hypothesis and the backup hypothesis.
Step 3: I draft a 3-line Slack message to the engineering team asking them to check a specific thing — specific, not vague.

After all 3 steps, give feedback in EXACTLY this format:
WHAT WORKED: [bullets]
WHAT MISSED: [bullets]
INDIA-SPECIFIC NOTE: [one insight about how India's regional holiday calendar creates diagnostic complexity that national-level dashboards hide — and why a Swiggy PM benefits from knowing the North Indian festival calendar]
COACHING NOTE: [one specific improvement tip]

Begin with: 'Good. Your primary hypothesis is regional holiday demand reduction for Delhi on January 8. Tell me: if you pull the data for January 15 (Pongal / Makar Sankranti day itself) and Delhi orders are completely normal — what does that tell you about your hypothesis? And what is your backup hypothesis?'

assessmentTask.taskDescription: Build a full 2-level RCA tree for a Swiggy dashboard anomaly using 7 data signals, identify the most likely root cause, and list 3 prioritised data checks with the reasoning behind each.
assessmentTask.rawMaterial:
You are an APM at Swiggy. It is Monday 9am. The weekly dashboard shows the following vs the same Monday last week:

Signal 1: Total orders — down 18% week-on-week
Signal 2: App sessions (opens) — flat (0% change)
Signal 3: Overall conversion rate (sessions to completed orders) — down 14%
Signal 4: Cart abandonment rate — no change (flat)
Signal 5: Drop-off at the payment step — up 22%
Signal 6: Android order volume — down 31%
Signal 7: iOS order volume — flat (0% change)

Additional context:
- Engineering shipped a new version of the Android app (v8.4.1) last Thursday
- No new marketing campaigns are running this week
- No known competitor promotions this week
- Weather is normal in all top cities

Your deliverables:

A. RCA TREE: Build a 2-level MECE tree. Top-level branches must cover all possible causes for the order drop given the 7 signals above. At the second level, expand at least 2 branches into sub-causes. Label where each of the 7 signals provides evidence (i.e., which branch does each signal help rule in or rule out?).

B. ROOT CAUSE HYPOTHESIS: State your single most likely root cause in one specific sentence. Include: what broke, on which platform, caused by what event, affecting which funnel step.

C. THREE NEXT DATA CHECKS: List 3 specific data checks you would run in order of priority. For each: state exactly what you would ask the analyst to query, what result would confirm your hypothesis, and what result would reject it.
assessmentTask.outputFormat: Section A: An indented MECE tree with top-level branches, sub-branches, and signal labels at the leaf level (e.g., 'Signal 6 rules this branch in'). Section B: One specific hypothesis sentence. Section C: A numbered list of 3 data checks, each with — Query description (plain English) / Confirms hypothesis if: [result] / Rejects hypothesis if: [result].
assessmentTask.claudePrompt:
You are a senior PM reviewing my work on Day 9: Diagnosing Metric Drops and Root Cause Analysis.

I studied today:
- Metric drop diagnosis playbook: funnel isolation, platform split, geography split, time-of-day split — in that sequence
- Root cause analysis: 5 Whys technique, moving from symptom to root cause through targeted data checks
- How platform-specific drops almost always point to a code release or infrastructure issue on that platform

Real world context I should apply:
Swiggy Monday morning dashboard: total orders down 18%, sessions flat, conversion down 14%, cart abandonment flat, payment step drop-off up 22%, Android down 31%, iOS flat. Android app v8.4.1 shipped last Thursday.

MY TASK TODAY WAS: Build a 2-level RCA tree with all 7 signals placed, state the most likely root cause hypothesis, and list 3 prioritised data checks.

Here is my work:
[Pranav pastes his RCA tree, hypothesis, and 3 data checks here]

Review my work and give feedback in EXACTLY this format — do not change these headers:

WHAT I GOT RIGHT:
- [specific bullets with explanation]

WHAT NEEDS CORRECTION:
- [specific bullets with explanation of why — especially check whether the RCA tree is truly MECE, whether the hypothesis is specific enough to be testable, and whether the data checks would actually confirm or reject the hypothesis rather than just 'look at more data']

BLIND SPOTS:
- [things I did not consider at all — e.g., whether 'sessions flat' with 'conversion down' tells us something specific about where in the funnel the regression sits; whether the Android v8.4.1 release note would be the first thing to check before running any query; or whether 31% Android drop with flat iOS is already sufficient to form a strong hypothesis without additional data]

INDIA-SPECIFIC NOTE:
- [one insight about Android dominance in India's app ecosystem — India has an 85%+ Android market share among consumer internet users, which means an Android-specific regression has a disproportionately large impact on Indian product metrics compared to markets with more even iOS/Android splits — a 31% Android drop in India is not a minor platform issue]

OPEN POINTS FOR TOMORROW:
- [1-3 specific, actionable items only]

SCORE: [X/10]
REFORGE COMPETENCY: Fluency with Data — [Needs Focus / On Track / Outperform]
